text: Hello, I'm sharing a case of a recto sigmoid cancer. This was in a patient who, a relatively young man, recent coronary stenting and starting on anti-platelet therapy, which led to a GI bleed, subsequently recognized to be iron deficient anemia and a colonoscopy found a recto sigmoid cancer.

Hello, I'm sharing a case of a recto sigmoid cancer. This was in a patient who, a relatively young man, recent coronary stenting and starting on anti-platelet therapy, which led to a GI bleed, subsequently recognized to be iron deficient anemia and a colonoscopy found a recto sigmoid cancer. After discussions with cardiology about the appropriateness and timing of going to the OR, He was brought in.

The video shows peritoneal implants on the parietal surface of the colon and the edge of the redundant sigmoid colon, which is being prepared for resection due to bleeding risk under full anticoagulation.
The video briefly shows peritoneal spread on the parietal surface of the colon, with visible peritoneal implants on the edge of the redundant sigmoid colon.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, isolating the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) just proximal to the left colic artery. The vascular adventitia is carefully dissected to ensure the vessel sealer only contacts the IMA, minimizing the risk of bleeding.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, isolating the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) just proximal to the left colic artery. The vascular adventitia is carefully dissected to ensure the vessel sealer only engages the IMA without additional tissue.

The surgeon performs additional medial to lateral dissection, revealing a protective sponge placed in the surgical field. The redundant sigmoid colon requires minimal lateral mobilization, and the ureter is identified during the dissection.

The surgeon performs additional medial to lateral dissection, with a protective sponge visible in the surgical field. The redundant sigmoid colon requires minimal lateral mobilization, and the ureter is identified during the dissection.

The surgeon identifies peritoneal studding lateral to the tumor and notes two tattoos marking the tumor's location, with the distal tattoo below the peritoneal reflection. The surgeon initiates the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, checking for the ureter without stents, and proceeds with the right-sided TME.

The surgeon identifies peritoneal studding lateral to the tumor and initiates the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection. The distal tattoo is visible below the peritoneal reflection, marking the tumor's location. The surgeon checks for the ureter using ICG imaging before proceeding with the right-sided TME.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection in a narrow male pelvis with a fatty mesentery, closely following the back of the mesorectum. The dissection progresses to a challenging area with limited visibility, where robotic arms are utilized to improve exposure.
The surgeon performs posterior dissection in a narrow male pelvis, carefully hugging the back of the mesorectum amidst a fatty mesentery.

The surgeon navigates a challenging area, using robotic arms to lift the prostate and improve exposure. The anterior peritoneal reflection is visible as the surgeon widens the anterior space, utilizing suction as a retractor to clear smoke and fluid.

The surgeon lifts the prostate to expose the anterior peritoneal reflection, entering the space and widening it using suction as a retractor. This technique helps clear smoke and fluid while aiming to reach five to six centimeters distal to the tumor for a tumor-specific mesorectal excision. The pelvic rectum is notably long, extending below the peritoneal reflection.

The surgeon performs a tumor-specific mesorectal excision, working circumferentially around the rectum. The dissection is carried out five to six centimeters distal to the tumor, which is located below the peritoneal reflection. The surgeon clears the mesentery at this level and verifies the margin using a rigid proctoscope. A tattoo is visible on the rectal wall, marking the distal location relative to the tumor.

The surgeon continues circumferential dissection around the rectum, focusing on the anterior aspect. The mesentery is cleared at the level below the tumor, with a visible tattoo on the rectal wall marking the distal margin.

The surgeon clears the anterior mesorectum at the same level to ensure the stapler passes only through the rectal wall. Subsequently, the proximal mesentery is dissected from just proximal to the transection of the inferior mesenteric artery up to the chosen margin in the proximal sigmoid colon, avoiding the need to extend into the descending colon due to the redundancy of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon clears the anterior mesorectum at the same level, ensuring that the stapler will only pass through the rectal wall without encountering additional tissue.

The surgeon begins to take the proximal mesentery, starting from just proximal to the transection of the inferior mesenteric artery and extending to the chosen margin in the proximal sigmoid colon. The redundant sigmoid colon allows for adequate lymph node clearance without needing to extend into the descending colon. The mesentery is taken intracorporeally in preparation for intracorporeal anastomosis, with plans to assess perfusion shortly.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal anastomosis, taking the mesentery intracorporeally and assessing perfusion. A 45mm stapler is used to transect distally, as the 60mm stapler would not reach. The stapler is positioned vertically to complete the transection across the rectum, requiring an additional load for full coverage.

The surgeon uses a 45mm stapler to transect the distal rectum, requiring an additional load to fully cross the rectum due to the angle constraints. The proximal transection is performed next, with visible tissue demarcation indicating perfusion assessment. A fan and steel incision is made for specimen extraction to assess the distal margin before proceeding with hand-sewn anastomosis.

The surgeon divides the proximal section of the colon, observing the demarcation and checking perfusion. A second stapler is used to complete the transection. The specimen is extracted through a fan and steel incision to assess the distal margin before beginning a hand-sewn anastomosis.

The surgeon orients the proximal bowel with a 90-degree counterclockwise turn, aligning the staple lines vertically on both the rectal and proximal colon sides, ensuring no tension. The camera is repositioned for a better view, and the surgeon begins the second suture for the anastomosis, with one suture directed left and the other right on the inner row. The mucosa appears healthy. The surgeon uses a suture to lift the rectal side for better visualization and continues to retract the rectum below the peritoneal reflection using the fourth arm.

The surgeon orients the proximal bowel with a 90-degree counterclockwise turn, aligning the staple lines vertically on both the rectal and proximal colon sides. The camera is repositioned for a better view, and the surgeon begins the second suture for the anastomosis, with one suture directed to the left and the other to the right on the inner row. The mucosa appears healthy. The surgeon uses a suture to lift the rectal side out of the tunnel below the peritoneal reflection for improved visibility, employing the fourth arm for better retraction.

The surgeon brings the initial inter-row suture from the left to join with the one on the right at the anterior midline, preferring a central joining of sutures. The two sutures are tied together, and the original posterior outer row suture is used to reinforce the anterior suture line. The bowel caliber is wide, ensuring a widely patent anastomosis. A leak test is performed using a 19mm proctoscope, confirming hemostasis and the absence of bubbles.

The surgeon takes the initial inter-row suture on the left and joins it with the one on the right at the anterior midline, tying them together to create a central suture line. The posterior outer row suture is then used to reinforce the anterior suture line, ensuring the anastomosis is widely patent. A leak test is performed using a 19mm proctoscope, confirming the anastomosis is hemostatic and without bubbles.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis and performs a leak test, observing the bowel distend smoothly. A scope is inserted and passes through the anastomosis without any issues, indicating a successful procedure.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and performs a leak test, observing that the bowel distends well. A scope is inserted and passes through the anastomosis smoothly, indicating no issues.
